item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
YEAH
HOW DID I ONLY JUST FIND THIS
BEST SKIP

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: He’s a riddle, wrapped in an enigma, lovingly sprinkled with intrigue, and express mailed to Mystery, Alaska.

item.title: I remember reading this years ago?
item.content:encoded: Did it get a new number or am I completely nuts? Or was it just so good I remembered it as a classic without it actually being one?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Congratulations, Sean, you win this week's show. Your prize is an ancient Greek philosopher's speed control for a juvenile amphibian salamander…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
No vote yet, but I really want to upvote.

For me it haves two major problems. The first is that I would like a little more development on their "personalities". It doesn't need to be sapience or anything, but just a collapsible with some of the observable distinctions Mulligan's actions caused on them, or that influenced his choices on roles.

The second, and greatest of all, is the excessive sensibility of Dr McLaughling. I actually like sensitive personnel far more than I like the cruel ones, but in this case it was a bit too much. It is a Foundation professional, he should be used to sicker bastards. At least be able to read the average SCP entry but not flinch, even knowing the anomaly is nearby. A younger researcher or a more subtle response could be a better fit here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm loving your skips, bitter. This is a great way to establish an emotional connection to these animals, and also connect to some problems in society today.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I chose not to stick the designation before the description each time for aesthetic purposes. Not to be rude, but this seems like a very minor inconvenience- there are only thirteen numbers, after all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it but I would love clarification as to which is which. I got pissed that I had to count to see which was which for 1-13.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Full Throttle Axolotl hooked me, and the flier got my +1. Expect an Audio Adaptation of that sucker some day, it's gonna be so much fun to say that part.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The narrative meanders a little, but the sheer ridiculous fun of this skip is more than enough for a +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yes, that is correct.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You mean Mulligan right? McLaughlin was the doctor who was interviewing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Probably not

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I wonder what the new scps for the 4000-4999 will be.
could we be running out of ideas?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: To sell, eat, or just for the sick fun of it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1

I re-rated this after a second read-through.

+ Here's why
- Hide

I think on the first read-through I was really caught off-guard by the line "sentient." I assumed the axolotls were sapient, and I kept waiting for you to expand on their feelings and motivations and personalities. You teased us with the different personalities in the flyer but never delivered.

By removing the word "sentient" and me now realizing they're not sapient… they're just regenerating, dumb lizards. it kind of changes the whole tone of the piece. There's nothing special about their personalities, which means Mulligan just made the whole thing up entirely. And for some reason, I find -that- interesting. Like he finds immortal lizards, and rather than sell them to some pharmaceutical lab or government, he decides to try and make a buck off of a flea circus where he repeatedly explodes them over and over again. And he clearly feels something towards them, he took the time to name them and come up with these elaborate costumes and 2 bit "acts," but he's still got no problem exploding them.

It's amazing how a single word can change the whole tone and feel of the piece.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Grammar note: "per say" should be "per se", being a borrowed Latin phrase.

This is a pretty good read, though I'm still not entirely certain why the guy shot a salamander in public.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I didn't want to mention it, but that was just in response to Doctor Vaughn's comment below. Originally they were going to be sapient, with interviews and etc- but I opted for keeping it short and sweet. At some point I obviously just replaced 'sapient' with 'sentient' without much thought and didn't remove it until now.

So, no- they are not sapient, but since they are a living things able to feel and perceive, they are sentient by default. Sorry for the confusion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I noticed you removed the line that they were sentient… so are they sentient, or not? I guess I should ask, are they -sapient- or not? Like do they know they're contained?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
FULL THROTTLE AXOLOTL is my new favorite thing, honestly.

Minor thing, though, is it necessary to specify that the axolotls are sentient? I'm pretty sure that's just a thing that living creatures are by default.

Good article, though— Simple but effective. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I found the primary effect dull

Ah, well- there's not an awful lot I can do about that without a full rewrite. Thanks for checking it out anyways. :^)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
-1

Sorry, I didn't find this particularly interesting. I found the primary effect dull and I wasn't hooked at all by the emotional connection. Maybe if we got to know the subjects better, or there was some new insight on their condition… but a guy trying to make a buck off some immortal lizards doesn't do it for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is fun. I like the use of internal documentation and a lot of the presentation here just feels very slick. Also particularly enjoyed the well-written and unique containment procedures. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I've had a soft spot for axolotls since reading the poem from "Mad" decades ago. McLaughlin isn't getting half the kicking around he deserves, but it's good to see that the Foundation has given his unfortunate captives a good home.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
ITS FINALLY OUT

and don’t worry about the SCP-XXXXs I did the same thing with 3524 lol

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh my God, that is mortifying. I totally forgot. Thank you ever so much.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fixed the SCP-XXXXs for you!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
With special thanks to:

Zyn DrChandra Sly161
taylor_itkin and Leveritas for their help and support.

https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/Category:Ambystoma_mexicanum#/media/File:2leucistic_axolotls.jpg

The Intelligence Agency logo was taken from this page.
